item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it is owned by Leticia Wilson (as indicated here), with no indication it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Much like Gardeford said back in 2015, my first thought was that the death of the first child was a hole in the plot.
But now I'm imagining the scenario fully, and it all fits really well.

It had been particularly hot all week, and little Timmy had been bugging his parents relentlessly ever since he saw that commercial for the new "super slide" over at Wild Springs. So come Saturday, they relent. Everyone piles in the car bright and early so Timmy can be the first in line. He runs up the staircase, practically vibrating as he waits for the teenaged life guard to unlock the safety gate. When he finally gets permission, Timmy dives down the pipe head first with a resounding "WEEEEeeeeee". Then something goes wrong.
Instead of splashing into the pool a few seconds later, the lifeguard hears Timmy's surprised shouts echoing up the pipe. The kid got stuck! It happens sometimes, but usually only to the real porkers.
There's a procedure for this. The bored teen announces that the slide is temporarily closed and calls the office on his radio, speaking over the disappointed groan of all the other kids waiting in line.
He calls in again a few minutes later, when Timmy starts shouting about "It burns!". Sometimes the water doesn't flow right and there can be some nasty friction burns.
But nobody really started to panic until twenty minutes later when the kid stops screaming, and the guy they lowered on a rope comes back to say there is some sort of "meat wall" completely blocking the pipe.
That's when they called the fire department.
The cops got involved a few hours later when the "wall" vanished and a bunch of slime and gore spattered out into the pool.
The unidentified "government agents" in black suits didn't show up till the next day.
Wild Springs never got over the bad publicity from the "accident" and the owners filed for bankruptcy within the month.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Imagine the horror on the parents' faces when they see their █-year-old kid's digested remains being shat out of the pipe and landing in front of them.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Foundation contains anomalous entities, it does not attempt to destroy them.

Additionally, the water park is condemned anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Caught me off guard. Didn't think "yeah, that's gonna digest folk" when I saw the picture. Not entirely clear on why the Foundation doesn't just dismantle it, since it's out in the open, but still gets a +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "SCP-1829 was discovered four days after its installation at the "Wild Springs" water park, when a █-year-old child was digested and ejected in front of ██ civilians" because they all just waited 5 hours for the kid to be digested? no vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ooh, I missed this one since I read it last. A belated +1 and an emphatic 'Well done' from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cool idea, I wonder how it would react to a block of cannabis?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for bringing that to our attention. A few days ago we ran a conversion script that changed old image code to the new version. Apparently something went wrong here and some of the test logs disappeared for some reason. The edit's been reverted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: so what happened to the other test logs? they're mostly blank, or is it just me?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Haha, cool! Thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fun fact: I made a thing out of the article image by messing around with it a lot until something looked really nice. It's set up to be posted to my blog in five days, but y'all get to see it early because I love like you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's simple, horrifying, and gross, nothing more. And that's ok. +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dunno, it would be really hard to add something else to this without screwing over what you've already got. You could go either way on it.
An upvote from me for the sheer "wtf"ery.
And to be honest, I used to have a fear just like that too lol

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personally, I think it adds to the article the way the waterslide "gives up" on digesting certain things, and how ipecac syrup causes it to "get sick" - I think it gives it a bit more of a sentient feeling, even if it is just a waterslide.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, not -immediately-. But that is also the most important word in that sentence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…yeah, not so much. Thing is, if one is not going to do anything interesting with the test logs, one should not include said test logs in the article.

This is not to imply that the Foundation would not conduct these tests, it's just that they wouldn't be in the main article.

(Also we don't immediately make sacrificial offerings of d-class unless there is good evidence to suggest that we need to. Because that's just silly.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why would the Foundation assume it had the same limitations as a human stomach? We've got the proto-lizard, a tumor-ship, and a human-looking-thing that operates its own pocket dimension made of screaming. I'm more surprised we didn't immediately make a sacrificial offering of d-class.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea, and like others said, I really wouldn't mind if there was something else going on. No vote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one, it's certainly creative and very unusual, although I feel that you might be able to do more with the concept than has been done, and I can't help thinking that the test log may be a little too close to that of SCP-914.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a neat concept and and a decent start, but I would personally cut out the "Let's not do that again" comment at the end, as that's borderline LolFoundation.

Glad to see that you're setting out on your own rather than editing existing articles. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wrote an "SCP that makes things" and got as far as Raven Mackenzie telling me it was a stupid idea and that we already had those. Don't do an SCP that makes things if you value the Senior Staff having a good impression of you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Haha. Awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I understand what you are saying, but apparently several people like the test logs so I will be leaving them in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, though I actually don't feel like the test logs add much. "It can't digest generally indigestible things." Okay, so what?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Added iron shavings and ipecac syrup to the testing log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you all for your positive feedback! I couldn't have done it without you guys' help.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The changes have helped, IMO. Cautiously upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this one. I don't think it needs to be added on to, as a giant digestive system disguised as a water slide is already pretty creepy IMO. I'm imagining some kid, seeing this huge water slide, running up the stairs and jumping in, happily sliding to his ultimate fate of slow digestion in the bowels of a horrible monster. I love it, +1.

I also really like how it was a normal slide for a while, adding more of an air of mystery.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nothing at the moment, really. I'd be more interested in seeing someone else come up with an idea for this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It poops you out as well! That's the important part.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm of the opinion that any kind of hook would ruin the atmosphere this one has. It's running on wtf, and I'm okay with that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one for its simplicity. It's a waterslide that digests you, no more, no less.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconding the ipecac idea. The thought of a water slider vomiting a half digested still alive D class sounds terrifying.

By the way I absolutely love this one! +1 for me!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's a good idea! Thanks again for all your help.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I upvoted.

Not to echo what everyone else has said, but this can be polished a bit more. The test logs are a nice touch. Just as an idea, if you are testing the digestive functions, perhaps a test involving a hefty dose of laxatives or ipecac syrup to see what the response is. Observers will probably need to wear raincoats.

item.title: New testing log.
item.content:encoded:
Thanks again for the critique everyone! I have added a small testing log showing the limits of SCP-1829's digestion abilities (it will take extra hours to digest heavier, denser items - will give up if unable to digest in a certain amount of time, etc.). Please tell me if the testing log feels unnecessary, makes the page too long, etc.

EDIT: Also added tags. Can't believe I almost forgot to do that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This works differently than actual human digestion. Of course, this being anomalous, it doesn't have to work that way, but if you want to make it as similar to real human digestion as possible:

After digestion is complete (usually spanning 4 to 5 hours) the fleshy aperture will begin to dissolve, and the digested slurry will continue into a rough intestine, where it is further broken down by a caustic mucus produced by SCP-1829.

In human digestion the acid in the stomach denatures (unfolds) proteins. When the food then enters the intestines the acid is neutralized by sodium bicarbonate and enzymes are secreted to break down the proteins, fats and carbohydrates.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What do you have in mind? (Just wondering)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Meh. Needs a hook of some sort instead of just eating you, IMO.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've had something like that happen to me at a public waterpark.
Except it was a screw and not a razor blade, and instead of eaaaaugh, it tore off my trunks and gave me a nasty scratch.
I've worn underwear beneath my swim trunks ever since.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In case anyone is wondering, this was inspired by a fear I've had for a while of having a razorblade or something embedded in one of those tube waterslides, and its enclosed and nobody can see it, and you slide right in and… eaaaaugh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I added a little part about the initial discovery, and how it "got hungry" so to speak.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Doesn't really do much for me, but it isn't terrible. No vote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Becoming an "SCP that makes things" isn't necessarily a bad thing, as long as it's well written and what it makes doesn't seem shoehorned in or over-the-top, although we do have more than a few SCPs that eat people and create weird things…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! I will change the tone at the "dodgy" bits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted because I like it. The idea is good, although the tone is a bit dodgy at times ("effectively digesting the subject" "fleshy aperture" "violently ejected").

If you wanted to add a little something to this, why not imply that there are more out there? Either it's a) one giant starfish-like tube monster that's been dismembered (capable of having a new creature grow back from just an "arm" or whatever) and has for, whatever reason, been put into various waterslides or b) it's a parasite that latches onto existing waterslides, eventually growing around the interior of the slide (in this case, the entire slide isn't digesting, just the infected bit downward).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Only thing is, if I do that it'll become another "SCP that makes things". I do understand what you are saying though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This feels too much like a D & D monster. As others have said, it needs something. Maybe if the bone fragments made some type of message, or a newborn made of the genetic material fed into the SCP came out, or anything that makes this different than "disguised monster that eats you".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What do you have in mind to make it *pop* a bit more?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Currently adding some bits and bobs

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just wondering, what do you think would be good as a hook?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As has been said before, you need a spark. No vote yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Echoing what the others said. The writing is workmanlike, which works for this sort of article, and the idea isn't half bad either, but it just doesn't seem to go anywhere. I think some additions could really make this article shine: perhaps an interview with a survivor, some interesting bit of back story (was the management of the park aware of this? Did it let it eat people for some reason?), even an experiment log on how it digest different things.

You can make this work, I think, if you find the right hook for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea is fantastic, but there's no hook. You don't really go anywhere with it, the descriptions are kind of lackluster, and it comes off as disappointing overall, because this is a fantastic idea you could have done a whole lot with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's certainly not bad, just boring. The idea is too plain for me, but since it's otherwise well-written, no vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not bad, but not particularly good either. No vote, but I see potential.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmmm… it's not bad, you have the tone down pretty good, and I like the idea… but there doesn't really seem to be anything interesting. Even something like an incident log or an interesting addendum would spice this up a little more. No vote for now.
